phase_metadata.description:
Learn to protect data integrity through Encapsulation
and organize code effectively using Java Packages.

learning_objectives:
Implement data hiding using private fields and public
methods

Master the four access modifiers: public, private, protected, and
default
Organize Java classes into logical package structures

Understand
the 'import' statement and naming conventions

Learn the basics of JAR files
for code distribution
introduction:
Encapsulation is the process of wrapping data (variables) and
code (methods) together as a single unit. It acts as a protective shield that
prevents data from being accessed by code outside this shield. Packages, on the
other hand, provide a mechanism to group related classes, acting like folders on
a computer to keep our project organized and avoid naming conflicts.

concept_explanation.what_is_it:
Encapsulation is the technique of making
class fields private and providing access via public getter and setter methods.
Packages are namespaces that organize classes and interfaces.
concept_explanation.why_do_we_need_it:
Encapsulation ensures data integrity by validating input
before changing a variable. Packages prevent 'namespace collisions' (two classes
with the same name) and make large projects manageable.
concept_explanation.how_it_works:
By
using the 'private' keyword, we hide the internal state of an object. To allow
controlled access, we provide 'getter' (read) and 'setter' (write) methods.
Packages use the file system directory structure to map class names.
concept_explanation.access_modifiers:
1. private: Accessible only within the same class. 2.
default (no keyword): Accessible only within the same package. 3. protected:
Accessible in the same package and by subclasses in different packages. 4.
public: Accessible from anywhere.
concept_explanation.syntax_explanation:
Packages must be
declared at the very top of a Java file. Imports follow the package
declaration.

syntax_breakdown.package:
A keyword used to group related classes. It
must be the first line of code.
syntax_breakdown.import:
A keyword used to make classes from
other packages available in the current file.
syntax_breakdown.private:
An access modifier
that restricts visibility to the containing class only.
syntax_breakdown.public:
An access
modifier that allows visibility to all other classes.
syntax_breakdown.protected:
An access
modifier that allows visibility to the package and subclasses.
syntax_breakdown.this_keyword: Used inside setters to distinguish between class fields and method parameters.

rules_and_key_points: A class can only have one package declaration.

Package names should be lowercase and follow reverse domain naming (e.g.,
com.google.search).

Fields should almost always be private to maintain
encapsulation.
Getters and setters must be public to be useful.

The
default access modifier has no keyword.

Standard Java classes (like String)
are in java.lang, which is imported automatically.

Static imports allow
accessing static members without the class name.

A JAR (Java ARchive) file is
a compressed collection of compiled .class files.

Subclasses can access
protected members of the parent even if in a different package.

Encapsulation
is also known as 'Data Hiding'.

code_example_1.title:
Standard
Encapsulation
code_example_1.description:
A BankAccount class where the balance is
protected from direct modification and validated via a setter.

Demonstrating how different
access levels work across classes.
code_example_1.output:
Deposited: $500.0
Current Balance: $1500.0
Name: John
Hobby:
Coding
Points: 100
Age is: 25
code_example_1.step_by_step_dry_run:
1. The program starts
in main. 
2. A BankAccount object is created on the Heap with
balance 1000.0.
3. The 'deposit' method is called. It checks if amount (500)
> 0.
4. Condition is true, so the private balance is updated (1000 + 500).
5.
The 'getBalance' method is called to safely retrieve the value.
6. The
AccessDemo class shows that public, protected, and default members are
accessible because AccessDemo and Person are in the same scope/package, but
'age' remains hidden.
code_example_1.memory_visualization:
Heap Memory: Contains the
BankAccount object. Inside that object, the field 'balance' exists. 
Stack
Memory: Contains the 'account' reference variable. 
Control Flow: Even though
'account' is on the stack, it cannot reach 'balance' directly. It must pass
through the 'deposit()' method frame on the stack, which acts as a gatekeeper to
the Heap data.
real_world_use_cases:
Financial Systems: Preventing
unauthorized balance updates.

API Libraries: Hiding complex internal logic
while exposing only simple methods to users.

Project Structure: Organizing a
'User Management' package vs an 'Inventory Management' package in an ERP
system.

Security: Protecting sensitive user data like password hashes within
a User object.
common_mistakes:
Making fields 'public' just to avoid
writing getters and setters.

Forgetting that 'default' access (no keyword)
does not allow access in different packages, even for subclasses.

Trying to
put the package declaration after the import statements.

Using '.' imports
excessively, which can lead to naming conflicts between different packages.

Not using 'this' keyword in setters, leading to shadowed variables (setting the
parameter to itself instead of the field).
best_practices:
Always use
the 'private' modifier for class variables.

Provide getters and setters only
when necessary (Read-only objects only need getters).

Avoid using the default
package; always define a package name.

Keep one public class per file and
name the file after that class.

faq.question:
What is the
difference between Encapsulation and Data Hiding?
faq.answer:
Data hiding
(using private) is a part of encapsulation. Encapsulation is the broader concept
of grouping data and methods together and controlling access to that unit.

faq.question: Can a class be private?
faq.answer:
Top-level classes cannot be
private; they can only be public or default. However, inner classes (classes
inside other classes) can be private.

faq.question: What is a JAR file?
faq.answer:
A JAR (Java ARchive) file is a package file format used to aggregate
many Java class files and associated metadata and resources into one file for
distribution.

code_example_1.summary:
Phase 8 covered the crucial concepts of
Encapsulation and Packages. We learned how to protect object data using access
modifiers and how to provide controlled access through getters and setters. We
also explored how packages help in organizing code and managing visibility
across different parts of a Java application.
key_takeaways:
Public is for everyone, Private is
for the class only.

Protected is for family (subclasses) and neighbors
(package).

Packages prevent
naming conflicts and organize code like folders.
code_example_1.preview_of_the_next_topic:
In Phase 9, we will learn about Exception
Handling, which allows our programs to handle errors gracefully without
crashing.

questions.question:
Which access modifier provides the highest level of data
protection?
questions.answer:
The 'private' access modifier provides the highest
protection as it restricts access to only the class where the member is
defined.

questions.question: Explain the difference between 'default' and 'protected' access modifiers.
questions.answer:
'Default' (no keyword) allows access only within the same package.
'Protected' allows access within the same package AND by subclasses even if they
are in different packages.

questions.question:
How do you create a 'Read-Only' class in Java using
encapsulation?
questions.answer:
Make all fields 'private' and 'final', and provide
only 'getter' methods. Do not provide any 'setter' methods so the object's state
cannot be changed after creation.

questions.question:
If class A is in package 'p1' and class B
(subclass of A) is in package 'p2', can B access a 'default' method of A? Why?
questions.answer:
No. Class B cannot access a default method of A because default
access is strictly limited to the same package. Even though B is a subclass,
'default' is more restrictive than 'protected'.

questions.question:
Correct the following setter: public
void setName(String name) { name = name; }
questions.answer:
The issue is variable
shadowing. Use the 'this' keyword to refer to the class field: 'public void
setName(String name) { this.name = name; }'.

questions.question:
What is the error in a Java file that
starts with an 'import' statement followed by a 'package' statement?
questions.answer:
The 'package' statement must be the very first non-comment line in a Java file.
If an 'import' appears before 'package', the code will not compile.

questions.question:
Write a class
'Student' with encapsulated fields 'name' and 'grade'. Ensure 'grade' can only
be set if it is between 0 and 100.

Demonstrate the use of a 'static import'
to use 'Math.sqrt' and 'Math.PI' without the 'Math.' prefix.

mcqs.question:
Which keyword is used to import a specific
class into a file?

What is the primary benefit of
encapsulation?

options.B:
Data security
and control over how data is modified
options.D: Reduction in the number of classes needed

mcqs.question:
Predict Output: Class A { private int x = 5; } Class B { main { A obj = new
A(); System.out.print(obj.x); } }

If
no access modifier is specified, what is the default visibility?
Which of these is a valid package declaration?

options.B:
The package statement must come before the import
statement
options.D:
Class name should be in
lowercase

mcqs.question:
Which modifier allows a member to
be accessed by a subclass in a different package?

mcqs: Make fields private and provide getters and setters with validation
mcqs.C: Make fields protected and put all other classes in the same package
